elements.sent-2411-001: Mean-field limits have been used now as a standard tool in approximations, including for networks with a large number of nodes.
elements.sent-2411-002: Statistical inference on mean-filed models has attracted more attention recently mainly due to the rapid emergence of data-driven systems.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, studies reported in the literature have been mainly limited to continuous models.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we initiate a study of statistical inference on discrete mean-field models (or jump processes) in terms of a well-known and extensively studied model, known as the power-of-$L$ ($L\geq 2$), or the supermarket model, to demonstrate how to deal with new challenges in discrete models.
elements.sent-2411-005: We focus on system parameter estimation based on the observations of system states at discrete time epochs over a finite period.
elements.sent-2411-006: We show that by harnessing the weak convergence results developed for the supermarket model in the literature, an asymptotic inference scheme based on an approximate least squares estimation can be obtained from the mean-field limiting equation.
elements.sent-2411-007: Also, by leveraging the law of large numbers alongside the central limit theorem, the consistency of the estimator and its asymptotic normality can be established when the number of servers and the number of observations go to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-008: Moreover, numerical results for the power-of-two model are provided to show the efficiency and accuracy of the proposed estimator.
elements.sent-2411-009: The origins of mean-field theory trace back to the pioneering works of Curie \cite{Curie1895 and Weiss \cite{Weiss1907 in magnetism and phase transitions.}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Since then, this theory has expanded across a wide array of fields to study interacting particle systems, including statistical physics \cite{Daw83, Gart88, McKean66, McKean66(2) , biological systems \cite{Daw2017, Mel+Ben2015 , communication networks \cite{Ben+Leboud2008, Graha2000, Gra+Mel93, Gra+Mel95 , and mathematical finance \cite{Giesecke+al2015, Kley+Klu2015 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Moreover, the application of mean-field theory in queueing systems is traced back to the work of Dobrushin and Sukhov \cite{Dobru76 and has since proliferated due to its many benefits, see, e.g., \cite{Daw+Zhao2005, Daw+Zhao2019, Mitz96, Dobru96 for further developments.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Indeed, in stochastic service systems, particularly those involving multiple parallel queues, load balancing is commonly applied to enhance performance by shortening queues, reducing wait times, and increasing throughput.
elements.sent-2411-013: This balancing mechanism effectively modifies the input-output dynamics to improve the system's quality of service.
elements.sent-2411-014: When such systems are viewed as interacting systems, mean-field theory becomes a natural framework to study their behavior.
elements.sent-2411-015: By using mean-field analysis, the performance of large systems can be evaluated by examining their limiting behavior as the system size approaches infinity.
elements.sent-2411-016: In particular, the limit often reduces when solving a deterministic system known as the mean-field limit, which corresponds to a McKean-Vlasov-type stochastic differential equation (SDE) solution.
elements.sent-2411-017: In McKean-Vlasov SDEs, the coefficients depend on both the process itself and its distribution, forming a class of non-linear SDEs.
elements.sent-2411-018: The study of such equations was initiated by McKean \cite{McKean66 , inspired by Kac's work in kinetic theory \cite{Kac56 .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Although extensive literature exists on mean-field interacting systems, research on their statistical inference has only gained attention in recent years.
elements.sent-2411-020: The pioneering work in this area is Kasonga's seminal paper \cite{Kasonga90 , which addressed parameter estimation for interacting particle systems modeled by It\^{o SDEs through a maximum likelihood approach.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: After Kasonga's work, interest in the topic waned for nearly two decades before reemerging with significant contributions, as seen in \cite{AMORINO2023, belo2023, Bishwal2011, Genon+al2021, Dell+Hoff2022, SHARROCK2023 and references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Since then, the field has grown steadily, establishing itself as a crucial area of research.
elements.sent-2411-023: This renewed interest can be attributed to novel applications of mean-field theory and the rise of new technologies enabling access to massive datasets generated by systems of interacting agents.
elements.sent-2411-024: To date, most statistical inference studies on mean-field models, such as those mentioned above, focus on interacting diffusion systems, with limited research on statistical inference for mean-field systems with jumps.
elements.sent-2411-025: Notable exceptions include \cite{Del+Four2016 and \cite{Liu2020 , where the authors proposed asymptotic estimation for the Bernoulli interaction parameter in a system of interacting Hawkes processes as both the number of particles and time approach infinity.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: In particular, as discussed in \cite{Zhao2022 , statistical inference in mean-field queueing models remains largely unexplored, despite statistical inference in queueing systems being an active area of research.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The reader can consult for example \cite{Asanj+al2021 where a comprehensive survey on parameter and state estimation for queueing systems across various estimation paradigms was provided, yet it does not address statistical inference for mean-field queueing systems.}
elements.sent-2411-028: To fill this gap, we propose in the current paper a statistical inference scheme for the parameters governing a specific mean-field queuing system, namely the supermarket model, also known as the power of $L \geq 2$ choices.
elements.sent-2411-029: Thus, to the best of our knowledge, our current proposal represents a novel contribution in this area.
elements.sent-2411-030: The supermarket model was independently introduced by Vvedenskaya et al. \cite{Dobru96 and Mitzenmacher \cite{Mitz96 .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: It represents a system of $N$ parallel identical queues, each served by a single server with a service rate $\nu$ and infinite buffer capacity.
elements.sent-2411-032: Tasks arrive at a rate of $N\lambda$; each task is allocated $L$ queues chosen uniformly at random among the $N$ and joins the shortest one, with ties resolved uniformly.
elements.sent-2411-033: All events in this system are independent.
elements.sent-2411-034: In particular, \cite{Dobru96 and \cite{Mitz96 studied the asymptotic behavior of the system as the number of servers becomes large, showing that the process associated with queue lengths converges to a deterministic limit represented by an infinite system of ordinary differential equations (ODEs).}}
elements.sent-2411-035: This model and its extensions have since become widely studied due to its theoretical and practical importance; see, e.g., \cite{Graha2000, Budhi+Fried2019, Budh+Mukh+Wu2019 and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, as noted, the statistical inference for this model remains unexplored, which is the focus of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-037: We propose a statistical inference scheme to estimate the arrival and service rates in a supermarket model based on aggregate data obtained from discrete observations of a moment of the system over a finite period.
elements.sent-2411-038: To this end, we propose to exploit the ODE obtained at the mean-field limit to construct an approximate least square estimator (LSE).
elements.sent-2411-039: Then, using the law of large numbers and the central limit theorem established in the literature, we show that the proposed estimator is consistent and asymptotically normal as the number of servers and observations grows large.
elements.sent-2411-040: In addition, we test our estimator on synthetic data obtained by simulation which shows the accuracy of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-041: It is worthwhile to provide the following remark: An intriguing general approach to statistical inference for mean-field systems was proposed in \cite{Giesecke+al2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: This approach leverages a ``misspecified" or limiting model, created by approximating the system through large-systems asymptotics, incorporating the law of large numbers and central limit theorem.
elements.sent-2411-043: This enables constructing an approximate likelihood function, evaluated against the data generated by the true model.
elements.sent-2411-044: The estimator is then obtained by maximizing this approximate likelihood function.
elements.sent-2411-045: A key advantage of this method is that the approximate likelihood has a conditionally Gaussian structure, due to the central limit theorem, which allows for efficient numerical evaluation of the estimator.
elements.sent-2411-046: Although one might consider using a similar method to estimate parameters for the supermarket model, the complexity of the approximate likelihood for this model complicates the analysis of the estimator's asymptotic properties.
elements.sent-2411-047: This difficulty motivates the adoption of an alternative approach, specifically, an approximate LSE scheme.
elements.sent-2411-048: The rest of the paper is organized as follows: First, in Section \ref{model , we recall the supermarket model and introduce the appropriate notations.}
elements.sent-2411-049: We also review some well-known asymptotic results about the model, including a new technical result in Proposition \ref{explicit-sol that will be used in the sequel.}
elements.sent-2411-050: In Section \ref{stat-inf , we introduce the inference scheme along with our LSE and prove both the consistency and asymptotic normality of the estimator.}
elements.sent-2411-051: To facilitate the reading, we put all the long proofs in the appendices.
elements.sent-2411-052: Section \ref{num-exp provides simulations demonstrating that our estimator accurately predicts the system parameters and validates the asymptotic normality result.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Finally, in Section \ref{conc , we present conclusions and open questions, followed by the bibliography.}
elements.section-2411-002: Queuing network with selection of the shortest queue among several servers
elements.sent-2411-054: We start by recalling the supermarket model, first introduced separately in \cite{Mitz96 and \cite{Dobru96 .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Consider a network with $N$ identical queues, each with a single server of service rate $\nu$ and an infinite buffer.
elements.sent-2411-056: Tasks arrive at rate $N\lambda$, and each task is allocated $L$ queues with uniform probability among the $N$ servers and elects to join the shortest one, ties being resolved uniformly.
elements.sent-2411-058: All these random events are independent.
elements.sent-2411-059: Let $X^N_i(t)$ denote the length of the $i$-th queue at time $t$ and define the empirical measure process \begin{equation}\varrho^N_j(t):=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N\mathds{1}_{\{X_i^N(t)=j\}},\quad j=0,1,\ldots,\end{equation} which takes values in the space $\mathcal{P (\mathbb{Z _+)$ of probability measures on $\mathbb{Z _+=\{0,1,2,\dots\}$ identified with the infinite-dimensional simplex \begin{equation}S:=\bigg\{s=\{s_j\}_{j\inZ_+}\inR^{Z_+}_+:\sum_{j=0}^{\infty}s_j=1\bigg\},\end{equation} where $\mathbb{R _+$ is the set of all non-negative real numbers.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: Throughout this paper, we fix a constant $T>0$.
elements.sent-2411-063: Let $\mathcal{D ([0,T],\mathcal{S )$ be the Skorokhod space of c\`adl\`ag functions defined on $[0,T]$ with values in $\mathcal{S}$, equipped with the usual Skorokhod topology.}}

elements.section-2411-003: Statistical inference of the supermarket model
elements.sent-2411-082: Suppose the service and arrival rates, \(\nu\) and \(\lambda\), that govern the system are unknown.
elements.sent-2411-083: Our goal is to estimate these parameters using observations collected over a specific time interval \([0, T]\).
elements.sent-2411-084: The complexity of the system's dynamics makes brute-force Monte Carlo estimation computationally intensive, particularly as the number of nodes, \(N\), increases.
elements.sent-2411-085: Therefore, we propose developing an estimator that utilizes the weak convergence results outlined in Section \ref{model , specifically the law of large numbers presented in Theorem \ref{LLN-Budhi and the central limit theorem in Theorem \ref{CLT-Budhi .}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: In particular, we construct an approximate LSE based on the ODE given in \((\ref{ODE-LLN-Budhi )\).}
elements.sent-2411-087: We subsequently demonstrate that this estimator is consistent and asymptotically normal as both the system size and the number of observations tend to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-088: For convenience, we denote the vector of unknown parameters as \(\theta = (\lambda, \nu)\).
elements.sent-2411-089: Our objective is to estimate the parameter vector \(\theta\) that governs the dynamics of the power-of-two model based on observations collected over a finite time interval.
elements.sent-2411-090: Specifically, we assume that observations are not available for every server in the network; instead, they are gathered as an aggregate measure of the system.
elements.sent-2411-091: Collecting individual data for each server can be prohibitively costly in practice, particularly for large networks, which justifies our approach to data collection.
elements.sent-2411-092: In this context, we assume that the available data for inference includes observations of the empirical measure of the system, \(\varrho^N(t)\), over the finite time interval \([0, T]\) at \(m\) discrete points, defined as \(t_k = \frac{kT {m}\): \begin{equation}\varrho^N_j(t_k) := \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N\mathds{1}_{\{X_i^N(t_k) = j\}}, \quad j \in Z_+, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, m.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-093: The observed data is then represented as \begin{equation}D^{N,m} := \{\varrho^N(t_k): 1 \leq k \leq m\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-094: Thus, this dataset reflects a realization of the system governed by the true parameters, say \(\theta^* = (\lambda^*, \nu^*)\).

elements.sent-2411-152: In this paper, we considered the parameter estimation problem of the supermarket model.
elements.sent-2411-153: Based on an aggregate dataset, we constructed an approximate LSE by exploiting the law of large numbers together with the central limit theorem established for the model in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-154: Moreover, we proved the consistency together with the asymptotic normality of the estimator as both the size of the network and the number of observations go to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-155: Finally, we presented a numerical study where we tested our estimator against synthetic data obtained by simulating the power-of-two model highlighting our theoretical results.
elements.sent-2411-156: The current work is the first statistical scheme for mean-field queuing systems and opens a new perspective.
elements.sent-2411-157: One naturally aims to investigate the statistical inference problem for other models.
elements.sent-2411-158: For instance, one can investigate the approximate LSE approach to the model proposed in \cite{Budhi+Fried2019 for load balancing mechanisms in cloud storage systems which is a generalization of the supermarket model.}
elements.sent-2411-159: The established law of large numbers together with the central limit theorem make the approximate LSE approach used in the current work conceivable, provided that one can overcome the technical difficulty arising from the more complicated mean-field limiting equation.
elements.sent-2411-160: Another variation of the supermarket model for which one can study the parameter estimation problem is the one introduced in \cite{Budh+Mukh+Wu2019 in which the servers can communicate with their neighbors and where the neighborhood relationships are described in terms of a suitable graph.}
elements.sent-2411-161: Again, the limit as the number of servers goes to infinity was identified, which can be exploited to build a statistical scheme, however, no central limit theorem nor a stationary distribution was established, therefore, the asymptotic normality of the estimator cannot be obtained by the similar scheme used in the current paper.
elements.sent-2411-162: Another interesting open problem is the nonparametric estimation of the interaction kernel in general mean-field queuing systems studied in \cite{Daw+Zhao2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-163: Indeed, one can consider the exploitation of the limiting mean-field equation to build an estimator.
elements.sent-2411-164: However, contrary to our current proposal where the unknown parameters enter linearly in the mean-field limiting equation, in the nonparametric estimation one needs to deal with an optimization problem in function space.
elements.sent-2411-165: A potential avenue is to exploit the stationary distribution to build an estimator in the stationary regime and then investigate a justification for the interchange of limits $N\rightarrow\infty$ and $t\rightarrow\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-166: Finally, the problem of statistical inference for general mean-field models on discrete space remains open and, as mentioned in the introduction, very few references exist.. .
